title: The Monarch Butterfly Migration
description: For questions 9 – 16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only one word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: The monarch butterfly migration is (0) one of the most remarkable journeys in the natural world. Each autumn, millions of these delicate creatures travel up to 4,800 kilometres from Canada and the United States to spend winter in Mexico.||What makes this migration extraordinary is (9) that the butterflies making the journey have never been to Mexico before. They are following a route (10) that their great-great-grandparents took, relying entirely on instinct. Scientists still don't fully understand (11) how they navigate such vast distances, though it appears they use a combination of the sun's position and Earth's magnetic field.||The journey is perilous, (12) with many butterflies dying along the way due to exhaustion, predators, or bad weather. Those (13) that/which survive reach the oyamel fir forests in Mexico's mountains, where millions of monarchs cluster together on trees to stay warm during winter. The sight of trees covered (14) in orange and black butterflies is truly spectacular.||In spring, the butterflies mate and begin the return journey north. However, no single butterfly completes the round trip. Instead, (15) it takes four or five generations to complete the full migration cycle. The final generation of the year lives much longer (16) than the others, surviving up to nine months rather than just a few weeks, allowing them to make the incredible journey south.

example.text: The monarch butterfly migration is (0) of the most remarkable journeys.

questions.explanation: Conjunction 'that' introduces a noun clause after 'is'.
Relative pronoun referring to the migration route.
Interrogative adverb 'how' introduces an indirect question about navigation.
Preposition 'with' introduces information about the difficulties of the journey.
Relative pronoun referring to the butterflies that survive.
Expression 'covered in' indicates being covered in butterflies.
Formal subject pronoun: 'it takes' in a time expression.
Comparative: 'longer than the others' compares lifespans.
